case_name: Sunil Shreesail Killiket vs Subbraya on 18 July, 2024
court: IN THE COURT OF III ADDL.JUDGE AND MOTOR ACCIDENT CLAIMS TRIBUNAL, COURT OF SMALL CAUSES BENGALURU (SCCH-18)

compensation_breakdown.tribunal_amount: Rs.87,49,500/- (breakdown below as awarded by the Tribunal)
compensation_breakdown.interest: Interest at 6% p.a. on Rs.68,21,500 from date of petition till realization; no interest on Rs.19,28,000 (attendant charges Rs.17,28,000 and future medical Rs.2,00,000).
compensation_breakdown.notes: Breakdown: Loss of future income Rs.45,36,000; Medical expenses Rs.14,85,260; Nourishment Rs.1,00,000; Conveyance Rs.1,00,000; Attendant charges Rs.17,28,000; Pain & suffering Rs.1,50,000; Loss of amenities Rs.1,00,000; Permanent disability & loss of expectation of life Rs.50,000; Loss of marriage prospects Rs.3,00,000; Future medical expenses Rs.2,00,000. 60% of award to be deposited in bank for 5 years as ordered.

facts: On 09.01.2021 at about 5:30 p.m. petitioner Sunil Shreesail Killiket (aged about 24/25) was an occupant of car bearing registration No. KA-03-AC-5300 driven by Appu @ Bhagyodaya Shivapura which allegedly turned suddenly, went out of control and overturned near Ambha Dresses opposite MG Patel Hospital, Indi, Vijayapura. The petitioner sustained grievous spinal injuries (diagnosed with spinal cord injury, spondyloptosis C7-T1, underwent laminectomy and fusion and tracheostomy) and was treated as inpatient in multiple hospitals between January and March 2021. He claimed medical expenses of Rs.44,98,831/- (later examined) and total compensation of Rs.99,99,999/- against owner (Respondent No.1) and insurer (Respondent No.2) under Section 166 MV Act. The insurer denied liability alleging the driver had no valid driving licence and policy conditions were breached.
arguments_claimant: The claimant argued he suffered permanent and severe disability (medical evidence: 90% whole body, 100% functional disability), was unable to work and suffered loss of future earnings, medical expenses and other heads; he claimed monthly income of Rs.45,000 and sought full compensation across heads including attendant charges, future medical and loss of marriage prospects. He also argued that even if the insurer proves breach of policy, the insurer should be directed to pay the award to the third-party victim with liberty to recover from the owner (pay-and-recover).
arguments_respondent: Respondent No.2 (insurer) denied the claimant's case and contended the offending vehicle was not involved in the accident, challenged the claimed amounts as exorbitant, and contended the driver did not possess a valid driving licence and the owner breached fundamental policy conditions (thus insurer not liable to indemnify). The insurer relied on statutory/binding authorities to assert non-liability due to breach of policy terms.
ratio_decidendi: The Tribunal accepted that the insurer proved a breach of a fundamental condition of the policy because the driver did not possess a driving licence and therefore "the respondent No.2 is not liable to pay the compensation and he is absolved from its liability." However, the Tribunal held that the claimant is a third party who "cannot be made to suffer due to the fault of the owner of the offending vehicle and same can be recovered by the insurer from the owner." Applying precedents on equitable relief, the Tribunal therefore directed payment by the insurer under the doctrine of 'pay and recovery' and expressly ordered that "under the principle of 'Pay and Recovery' the respondent No.2 is directed to pay the award amount with accrued interest thereon to the petitioner ... by reserving liberty to the respondent No.2 to recover the same from the respondent No.1 in accordance with law." The legal effect is that proof of insurer's defence under Section 149(2) MV Act absolves indemnity liability but does not bar immediate relief to the third-party claimant; the insurer may later recover from the owner.
final_order: Petition allowed in part; award of Rs.87,49,500/- to the petitioner. Respondent No.1 (owner) held liable to pay; respondent No.2 (insurer) absolved of indemnity liability but directed, under 'pay and recover', to pay the award with accrued interest within 60 days and may recover the same from respondent No.1; interest at 6% p.a. awarded on Rs.68,21,500 from date of petition (no interest on Rs.19,28,000). Sixty percent of the award to be deposited in a bank for 5 years.
summary: This is a Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal judgment (Bengaluru, 2024) where Sunil Shreesail sustained severe spinal injuries in a January 2021 car accident and claimed compensation under Section 166 MV Act. The Tribunal found the car at fault and awarded Rs.87,49,500/- (reduced from the claim) across heads including future loss of income, attendant charges and medicals. Although the insurer proved a policy breach (driver without licence) and was absolved of indemnity liability, the Tribunal applied 'pay and recover' and directed the insurer to pay the award with limited interest and recover from the owner. The award includes an interest direction and protective deposit of 60% for five years.
embedding_summary: Sunil Shreesail Killiket v. Subbraya, III Addl. Judge & MACT, Court of Small Causes Bengaluru, M.V.C.No.5135/2021, judgment dated 18 July 2024. Accident on 09.01.2021 near Indi, Vijayapura involving car No. KA-03-AC-5300 driven by Appu; claimant sustained severe cervical spinal cord injury (C7-T1 spondyloptosis), treated at Vedanta, Vasudeva and Sparsh Hospitals. Claim under Section 166 Motor Vehicles Act 1988 for Rs.99,99,999; insurer (Bajaj Allianz) pleaded driver had no valid licence (charge sheet: IPC 279, 337, 338; MV Act Section 3 r/w 181) and breached policy; tribunal found breach but applied 'pay and recover'. Tribunal awarded Rs.87,49,500 (loss of future income Rs.45,36,000; medical Rs.14,85,260; attendant Rs.17,28,000; other heads) and directed insurer to pay award with liberty to recover from owner; interest 6% p.a. on Rs.68,21,500 from date of petition; notional income fixed at Rs.15,000/month for 2021, future prospects 40%, multiplier 18. Key doctrines: pay-and-recover, insurer absolved on breach but must advance compensation to third party, application of Pranay Shethi/Pranay Sethi principle for future prospects.
legal_principles: Insurer may be absolved of indemnity liability for breach of policy (unauthorised/unlicensed driver) but third-party victim cannot be made to suffer: apply 'pay and recover' to direct insurer to pay award and recover from owner.
Where income evidence is absent, adopt notional income — Tribunal adopted Rs.15,000/month for year 2021 (Karnataka SLSA/Lok Adalath reference).
Future prospects (additive) are applicable in permanent disability cases; applied 40% for a 25-year-old (Pranay Shethi / Pappu Deo Yadav principle).
Use of multiplier method for future loss of earnings — multiplier 18 applied for age 25 with 100% functional loss.
Attendant charges and future medicals can be awarded for life; attendant charges assessed by monthly rate and multiplier.
Interest on awarded compensation follows High Court guidance and Section 34 CPC principles where applicable; awarded 6% p.a. on the compensable portion (excluding attendant and future medical heads).
sections_cited: Section 3 read with Section 181 Motor Vehicles Act 1988
Order I Rule 10 Civil Procedure Code, 1908
cases_cited: Raj Kumar Vs. Ajay Kumar and another, (2011) 1 SCC 343
Pappu Deo Yadav Vs. Naresh Kumar and Others, AIR 2020 SCC 4424
National Insurance Company Limited Vs. Pranay Shethi, Spl. Leave (Civil) No.25590 of 2014 (reported in 2017(2) MACR 1514 (SC))
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. Vs. Sujata Arora and others, 2013 (3) T.A.C. 29 (S.C.)
Pappu & Others Vs. Vinod Kumar Lamba & Another, (2018) 3 SCC 208
Shamanna and another Vs. The Divisional Manager Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. and Ors., 2018 AIAR (Civil) 805
Amrit Paul Singh & Anr. Vs. TATA AIG General Insurance Company Ltd. & Ors. (2018 SAR (Civil) 768)
Perminder Singh Vs. New India Assurance Company Ltd. and others, (2019 (7) SCC 217
Kajal Vs. Jagadish Chand and others, AIR 2020 SC 776
V. Mekala Vs. M. Malathi and another, Civil Appeal No.4880/2014 (decided 25.4.2014)
Vijay Ishwar Jadhav and others Vs. The Divisional Manager, Bajaj Allianz General Insurance Co. Ltd., M.F.A.13390/2014 clubbed with M.F.A.25107/2013 (Karnataka High Court, 07.03.2018)
bm25_text: Sunil Shreesail Killiket vs Subbraya on 18 July, 2024 IN THE COURT OF III ADDL.JUDGE AND MOTOR ACCIDENT CLAIMS TRIBUNAL, COURT OF SMALL CAUSES BENGALURU (SCCH-18) 2024 On 09.01.2021 at about 5:30 p.m. petitioner Sunil Shreesail Killiket (aged about 24/25) was an occupant of car bearing registration No. KA-03-AC-5300 driven by Appu @ Bhagyodaya Shivapura which allegedly turned suddenly, went out of control and overturned near Ambha Dresses opposite MG Patel Hospital, Indi, Vijayapura. The petitioner sustained grievous spinal injuries (diagnosed with spinal cord injury, spondyloptosis C7-T1, underwent laminectomy and fusion and tracheostomy) and was treated as inpatient in multiple hospitals between January and March 2021. He claimed medical expenses of Rs.44,98,831/- (later examined) and total compensation of Rs.99,99,999/- against owner (Respondent No.1) and insurer (Respondent No.2) under Section 166 MV Act. The insurer denied liability alleging the driver had no valid driving licence and policy conditions were breached. The claimant argued he suffered permanent and severe disability (medical evidence: 90% whole body, 100% functional disability), was unable to work and suffered loss of future earnings, medical expenses and other heads; he claimed monthly income of Rs.45,000 and sought full compensation across heads including attendant charges, future medical and loss of marriage prospects. He also argued that even if the insurer proves breach of policy, the insurer should be directed to pay the award to the third-party victim with liberty to recover from the owner (pay-and-recover). Respondent No.2 (insurer) denied the claimant's case and contended the offending vehicle was not involved in the accident, challenged the claimed amounts as exorbitant, and contended the driver did not possess a valid driving licence and the owner breached fundamental policy conditions (thus insurer not liable to indemnify). The insurer relied on statutory/binding authorities to assert non-liability due to breach of policy terms. The Tribunal accepted that the insurer proved a breach of a fundamental condition of the policy because the driver did not possess a driving licence and therefore "the respondent No.2 is not liable to pay the compensation and he is absolved from its liability." However, the Tribunal held that the claimant is a third party who "cannot be made to suffer due to the fault of the owner of the offending vehicle and same can be recovered by the insurer from the owner." Applying precedents on equitable relief, the Tribunal therefore directed payment by the insurer under the doctrine of 'pay and recovery' and expressly ordered that "under the principle of 'Pay and Recovery' the respondent No.2 is directed to pay the award amount with accrued interest thereon to the petitioner ... by reserving liberty to the respondent No.2 to recover the same from the respondent No.1 in accordance with law." The legal effect is that proof of insurer's defence under Section 149(2) MV Act absolves indemnity liability but does not bar immediate relief to the third-party claimant; the insurer may later recover from the owner. Petition allowed in part; award of Rs.87,49,500/- to the petitioner. Respondent No.1 (owner) held liable to pay; respondent No.2 (insurer) absolved of indemnity liability but directed, under 'pay and recover', to pay the award with accrued interest within 60 days and may recover the same from respondent No.1; interest at 6% p.a. awarded on Rs.68,21,500 from date of petition (no interest on Rs.19,28,000). Sixty percent of the award to be deposited in a bank for 5 years.
